name: White Rice

identity.basisReason: Dry per-100g basis matches package labeling and avoids cooked-water distortion.

metricProvenance.header: USDA FoodData Central style raw unenriched long-grain white-rice entry, normalized to per 100 g edible portion
metricProvenance.saturated_fat_g: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.omega3_mg: derived trace fatty-acid field from the same source family; not a ranking-critical metric here
metricProvenance.polyunsaturated_fat_g: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.cholesterol_mg: plant food default, canonical expectation confirmed by source family
metricProvenance.starch_g: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.fibre_g: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.sugar_g: same canonical source family as header
metricProvenance.glycemic_index: withheld, too variety- and preparation-dependent to lock as a canonical fact for dry white rice
metricProvenance.essential_amino_acids_score: withheld, proxy score not yet backed by a locked amino-acid source table for this exact food form
metricProvenance.nonessential_amino_acids_score: withheld, proxy score not yet backed by a locked amino-acid source table for this exact food form
metricProvenance.bioavailability_percent: withheld, proxy estimate not production-safe for this plant food entry

pros.title: plain white rice is easy on many stomachs
pros.explanation: Its lower fibre and blandness make it a practical recovery or tolerance food for some people, which is more specific than generic convenience.

pros.title: cheap and easy to scale as a staple
pros.explanation: White rice earns real-world value from being affordable, shelf-stable, and easy to cook in large batches.

pros.title: neutral enough to pair with almost anything
pros.explanation: That flexibility helps explain why it stays common even when its standalone nutrition case is weak.

cons.title: refining strips out much of the fibre and micronutrient value
cons.explanation: That is the central reason white rice scores worse than intact whole grains.

cons.title: it is mostly starch with limited standalone nutrition
cons.explanation: The protein, fibre, and micronutrient payload are all modest for the calories.

cons.title: carb quality changes a lot with rice type and preparation
cons.explanation: That makes the exact glycemic story hard to lock honestly from a single dry-rice record.

scoreReadiness.summary: Identity, basis, and core raw nutrient fields are strong enough. Final grain ranking still needs a canonical GI handling rule for dry rice versus cooked rice reality.
blockers: glycemic_index intentionally withheld because preparation and variety matter too much
grain protein proxy metrics were removed until exact-source amino acid data is attached

sourceNotes: Identity locked to plain unenriched long-grain white rice, dry, per 100 g as sold.
Core nutrient values align with common USDA FoodData Central style entries for raw white rice.
Weak proxy metrics were removed rather than preserved as fake certainty.
